news_summary: Researchers have made groundbreaking advancements by solving a long-standing gallium mystery, innovating practical PhD pathways in China, applying reinforcement learning to quantum error correction, and observing special relativity effects on chemical bonds.
news_summary_fr: Des chercheurs ont réalisé des avancées révolutionnaires en élucidant un mystère de longue date concernant le gallium, en mettant en place des parcours de doctorat innovants et adaptés aux besoins pratiques en Chine, en appliquant l'apprentissage par renforcement à la correction d'erreurs quantiques et en observant les effets de la relativité restreinte sur les liaisons chimiques.
news_summary_es: Los investigadores han logrado avances revolucionarios al resolver un misterio sobre el galio que llevaba mucho tiempo sin resolverse, al impulsar nuevas vías prácticas para la formación de doctorado en China, al aplicar el aprendizaje por refuerzo a la corrección de errores cuánticos y al observar los efectos de la relatividad especial en los enlaces químicos.

articles.title: Scientists finally solved a 150-year-old gallium mystery
articles.summary: Scientists have rewritten the story of gallium after discovering that its unusual atomic bonds re-form at high temperatures, contradicting decades of accepted theory. The finding changes how researchers explain why the metal melts so easily and behaves unlike almost any other metal. Beyond solving a long-standing scientific mystery, the work could lead to advances in semiconductors, nanotechnology, and liquid metal engineering.
articles.content:
Nearly 150 years after gallium was first discovered and added to the periodic table, scientists at the University of Auckland have uncovered previously unknown details about the metal's atomic structure and behavior.

Gallium was discovered in 1875 by French chemist Paul Émile Lecoq de Boisbaudran. It is best known for its unusually low melting point, which allows a gallium spoon to melt in a cup of hot tea. The metal also plays an essential role in semiconductors and many modern electronic technologies.

The newly reported findings focus on how gallium behaves at the atomic level, revealing properties that challenge decades of scientific understanding.

A Strange Metal With Even Stranger Atomic Behavior

Gallium already stands out from most metals in several ways. Its atoms naturally pair up into `dimers,' meaning they exist as bonded pairs. It is also one of the few substances that is less dense as a solid than as a liquid, much like ice floating on water.

Another unusual feature is that gallium forms `covalent bonds,' in which atoms share electrons. This type of bonding is far more common in nonmetals than in metals.

Scientists had long believed those covalent bonds disappeared when gallium melted. However, the new study found that although the bonds vanish at the melting point, they unexpectedly return when the liquid is heated to even higher temperatures.

This discovery overturns a long accepted assumption and suggests a new explanation for gallium's remarkably low melting point. The researchers propose that when the bonds break apart, the resulting increase in entropy, a measure of disorder, frees the atoms and makes melting easier.

"Thirty years of literature on the structure of liquid gallium has had a fundamental assumption that is evidently not true," says Professor Nicola Gaston, of Waipapa Taumata Rau, University of Auckland and the MacDiarmid Institute for Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology.

Revisiting Decades of Research

The study was conducted by Dr. Steph Lambie, now a postdoctoral researcher at the Max Planck Institute for Solid State Research in Germany, Professor Nicola Gaston, and Dr. Krista Steenbergen of Victoria University of Wellington and the MacDiarmid Institute.

The breakthrough came while Lambie was completing a PhD at the University of Auckland and the MacDiarmid Institute. By carefully reviewing decades of published research and comparing measurements collected at different temperatures, Lambie assembled a more complete picture of gallium's behavior.

The findings were published in Materials Horizons in a paper titled "Resolving Decades of Debate: The Surprising Role of High-Temperature Covalency in the Structure of Liquid Gallium."

Why Understanding Gallium Matters

A better understanding of how gallium changes with temperature could benefit nanotechnology, where researchers manipulate matter at extremely small scales to create new materials with specialized properties.

Gallium is also valuable because it can dissolve other metals, making it useful for producing liquid metal catalysts and `self-assembling structures,' in which disordered materials spontaneously organize themselves into ordered forms.

In an earlier project, Gaston, Lambie, and Steenbergen used liquid gallium to crystallize zinc into intricate `snowflake' structures.

From Predicted Element to Modern Technology

Gallium was predicted before it was ever discovered. In 1871, Russian chemist Dmitri Mendeleev created the first periodic table by arranging elements according to increasing atomic numbers and intentionally left empty spaces for elements he believed had yet to be found. Gallium later filled one of those predicted gaps.

The metal is extracted from minerals and rocks such as bauxite and does not occur naturally in its pure form. Today, gallium is widely used in semiconductors, telecommunications equipment, LEDs, laser diodes, solar panels, high performance computing, the aerospace and defense industries, and as a safer alternative to mercury in thermometers.

Researchers are also investigating whether gallium could help identify signs of ancient life on Mars. Scientists at the University's School of Environment and Te Ao Mārama -- Centre for Fundamental Inquiry are studying whether the metal can preserve traces of past microbial life as a chemical `fingerprint.'

The name gallium comes from Gaul, the ancient Latin name for France, honoring the nationality of its discoverer.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough solving a 150-year-old mystery about gallium's atomic behavior, overturning decades of accepted theory. This discovery has broad implications for advancing semiconductors, nanotechnology, and liquid metal engineering, which can lead to meaningful technological progress benefiting society at large. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a substantial positive impact on science and technology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover new atomic behavior in gallium after 150 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gallium is a metal discovered in 1875 and is known for melting easily, even in a cup of hot tea. It is important in electronics and technology because of its special properties.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Auckland found that gallium’s atoms, which form pairs called dimers, behave differently at high temperatures than previously thought. They discovered that after gallium melts, the atomic bonds called covalent bonds disappear but then unexpectedly come back when the liquid is heated even more. This changes a long-held scientific belief about how gallium melts and behaves.

**Impact** – This finding helps explain why gallium melts so easily and shows that its atomic structure is more complex than scientists believed. Understanding these details can improve how gallium is used in making tiny electronic parts, new materials, and liquid metals that can change shape or assemble themselves. It also opens new possibilities in nanotechnology and could lead to better devices in electronics and other industries.

**What's next step** – Researchers will explore how this new knowledge about gallium’s atomic behavior can be used to develop advanced materials and technologies. They may also study if gallium can help detect signs of ancient life on Mars by preserving chemical traces.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have solved a 150-year-old mystery about gallium’s atomic bonds, revealing new behavior that can improve technology and materials science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un nouveau comportement atomique du gallium après 150 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le gallium est un métal découvert en 1875, connu pour fondre facilement, même dans une tasse de thé chaud. Il est important en électronique et technologie grâce à ses propriétés particulières.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Auckland ont découvert que les atomes du gallium, qui forment des paires appelées dimères, se comportent différemment à haute température que ce que l’on croyait. Ils ont trouvé qu’après la fusion du gallium, les liaisons atomiques appelées liaisons covalentes disparaissent, mais réapparaissent quand le liquide est encore plus chauffé. Cela change une croyance scientifique ancienne sur la fusion et le comportement du gallium.

**Impact** – Cette découverte aide à comprendre pourquoi le gallium fond si facilement et montre que sa structure atomique est plus complexe qu’on ne le pensait. Comprendre ces détails peut améliorer l’utilisation du gallium dans la fabrication de composants électroniques minuscules, de nouveaux matériaux et de métaux liquides capables de changer de forme ou de s’assembler seuls. Cela ouvre aussi de nouvelles possibilités en nanotechnologie et pourrait mener à de meilleurs appareils dans plusieurs industries.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier comment cette nouvelle connaissance du comportement atomique du gallium peut servir à développer des matériaux et technologies avancés. Ils pourraient aussi examiner si le gallium peut aider à détecter des traces de vie ancienne sur Mars en conservant des empreintes chimiques.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont résolu un mystère vieux de 150 ans sur les liaisons atomiques du gallium, révélant un nouveau comportement qui peut améliorer la technologie et la science des matériaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren nuevo comportamiento atómico del galio tras 150 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El galio es un metal descubierto en 1875, conocido por derretirse fácilmente, incluso en una taza de té caliente. Es importante en electrónica y tecnología por sus propiedades especiales.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Auckland encontraron que los átomos del galio, que forman pares llamados dímeros, se comportan de manera diferente a altas temperaturas de lo que se creía. Descubrieron que después de que el galio se derrite, los enlaces atómicos llamados enlaces covalentes desaparecen, pero vuelven a aparecer cuando el líquido se calienta aún más. Esto cambia una creencia científica antigua sobre cómo se funde y comporta el galio.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo ayuda a explicar por qué el galio se derrite tan fácilmente y muestra que su estructura atómica es más compleja de lo que los científicos pensaban. Comprender estos detalles puede mejorar cómo se usa el galio para fabricar partes electrónicas muy pequeñas, nuevos materiales y metales líquidos que pueden cambiar de forma o ensamblarse solos. También abre nuevas posibilidades en nanotecnología y podría llevar a mejores dispositivos en varias industrias.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores explorarán cómo este nuevo conocimiento del comportamiento atómico del galio puede usarse para crear materiales y tecnologías avanzadas. También podrían estudiar si el galio puede ayudar a detectar señales de vida antigua en Marte al conservar rastros químicos.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos resolvieron un misterio de 150 años sobre los enlaces atómicos del galio, revelando un nuevo comportamiento que puede mejorar la tecnología y la ciencia de materiales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of glowing, paired gallium atoms forming delicate, intertwined dimers that subtly dissolve and re-form amidst a softly swirling, molten metallic liquid, set against an abstract backdrop evoking scientific discovery and transformation through gentle gradients of silver, blue, and soft gold.

articles.title: Graduating without a thesis: meet the people getting ‘practical’ PhDs in China
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 09 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01242-zNature spoke to three students from China’s first cohort of PhD candidates who are allowed to graduate with products instead of papers.
articles.content:
The rail manufacturer Qingdao CRRC Sifang Rolling Stock worked with a Chinese PhD student to improve manufacturing techniques for its Cetrovo subway trains.Credit: Zhang Jingang/Feature China/Future Publishing via Getty

For most postgraduate students, a written thesis is a requirement for completing their studies. But in China, a law passed in 2024 is allowing some people to graduate with a practical achievement, such as an innovative product, technique or project, instead of following the conventional PhD route.

Since the law came into effect in January last year, more than 60 doctoral candidates have graduated with practical achievements, according to China’s Ministry of Education. The pathway is currently available only to people studying engineering courses, and it is part of China’s broad campaign to increase the numbers of ‘elite engineers’ in the nation, with the hope of overcoming technological bottlenecks. Around 50 postgraduate colleges have been established by universities across the nation since 2021 to support the elite-engineer campaign.

At these institutions, each student completes their training at both university and a company. They also have two supervisors — one in charge of their academic studies and the other helping them to gain hands-on experience. The engineering colleges offer both product-based and thesis-based graduation options, and students can choose which route to take.

First ‘practical PhDs’ awarded in China — for products rather than papers

Although universities in other nations also offer ‘industrial PhDs’, in which students work closely with a company, many of these degrees still require a written thesis. The product-based graduation route is expected to help train workers who are capable of solving real-life problems, says Zong Yingying, the dean of the College of Elite Engineers at the Harbin Institute of Technology in China.

A university and its partner company will agree on who owns the intellectual property for each resulting product, Zong explains. “But even if the university doesn’t get the intellectual property, it still benefits a lot from such a collaboration because it will get research funding and industry experts from the company, and the right to use their production lines.”

Nature spoke to three PhD graduates about their experiences taking part in the programme.

WU XIANGYANG: Railway engineer

Earned a PhD on intelligent manufacturing in rail transit systems at Southwest Jiaotong University in Chengdu, China.

Growing up in a small town on China’s east coast, I was obsessed with heavy machinery. My father worked for a company that made fish feed and he took many business trips to source machines. He often brought me with him on those trips. The memories of parts being welded together, the noise and smell of a busy workshop and the sight of machines being put together by a production line stuck with me.

I received a double bachelor’s degree in welding and software engineering in 2008. After that, I started working at the company Qingdao CRRC Sifang Rolling Stock, a manufacturer of rail vehicles based in Qingdao, China, at which I investigated ways to improve manufacturing techniques. But over the years, I began to feel the need to return to my studies to enhance my professional knowledge. That was why I started a master’s course in vehicle engineering in 2014, and then a PhD programme in mechanical engineering in 2022.

Wu Xiangyang was one of the first people to graduate with a practical PhD.Credit: Wu Xiangyang

My PhD course was provided jointly by Southwest Jiaotong University and Qingdao CRRC, which allowed me to pursue the PhD without resigning from my job. China’s railway manufacturing sector doesn’t lack advanced machines or skilled workers. What it lacks are processes that can coordinate the various machines in a system into the same production schedule so that it’s possible to build products safely and efficiently. That was what I set out to solve with my PhD project: a system of software and hardware that can plan, monitor and adjust production processes in rail-vehicle factories in real time.

I can’t share the exact details of my product owing to the confidentiality requirements of my course. But to develop it, I spent a lot of time on the factory floor to collect data from every step of the manufacturing process. Then, I built computer models on the basis of those data to find techniques that can solve specific problems. Finally, I returned to the factory to test and improve my product.

China could be the world’s biggest public funder of science within two years

One of the biggest challenges for me was that I had to do the bulk of my research in a factory, which was busy and noisy, and last-minute changes and uncertainty were common. To gather the data that I wanted, I often had to spend a lot of time searching for the best angle and location to record information from. And testing my product on a running production line was daunting. I was concerned about whether it would work, and whether it would lead to pauses in production. Any problems would potentially affect the work of everyone in the factory.

I am glad that I chose this graduation route. Compared with working out equations on paper or experimenting in a lab, what matters more to me as an engineer is whether innovations will work well in the real world. I will never forget the moment that the production line moved for the first time under the command of my system. That was the proudest day of my doctorate.

CUI GUANGZHANG: AI developer

Earned a PhD on intelligent computing systems at Zhejiang University in Hangzhou, China.

One day in 2003, I saw a shopkeeper in my hometown in eastern China having a video chat with someone on his computer using Tencent QQ, a Chinese instant-messaging service. I marvelled at how two people who were so far away from each other could talk face to face. From that moment, I knew that I wanted to do something with computers.

Developer Cui Guangzhang created an intelligent computing system called Yansheng.Credit: Cui Guangzhang

Twenty-two years later, I earned a PhD at Zhejiang University for developing an intelligent computing system called Yansheng, which supports the research, development and deployment of artificial-intelligence programmes. In a nutshell, the system coordinates a network of various types of computing equipment, enabling them to work together to train, fine-tune and deploy AI tools efficiently.

When I started my PhD studies in 2020, I intended to complete my degree by writing a thesis because that was the only option at the time. But as a PhD candidate in engineering, I often felt frustrated by this requirement. My heart was in solving practical issues, and I planned to go back to working outside academia after graduation. In January last year, when my supervisor told me about the alternative graduation option being introduced, I was ecstatic. But the road towards my graduation, which took place in December last year, was anything but smooth. Even though my product had been in development for a long time, I faced a path that no other students had walked before. I had to pass a product appraisal — equivalent to a thesis defence — made up of several steps, including a preliminary review conducted by faculty members at my university and some formal reviews from external specialists.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant educational innovation in China allowing PhD candidates in engineering to graduate with practical products instead of traditional theses. This policy promotes real-world problem solving, strengthens university-industry collaboration, and aims to overcome technological bottlenecks, benefiting society broadly. The story provides detailed context and examples of graduates' impactful projects, demonstrating substantial positive real-world impact and broad significance.
articles.personality_title: China launches practical PhDs to boost engineering innovation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In most countries, PhD students must write a detailed thesis to graduate. But since January 2025, China has allowed engineering PhD students to finish their studies by creating practical products or projects instead of writing theses. This change is part of a national effort to train top engineers who can solve real-world problems and help improve technology.

**What happened** – More than 60 students have already graduated under this new rule. These students study at both universities and companies, guided by two supervisors—one academic and one industry expert. For example, Wu Xiangyang, a railway engineer, developed a system to improve factory production for rail vehicles while continuing to work at a manufacturing company. Another graduate, Cui Guangzhang, created an intelligent computing system to help run artificial intelligence programs more efficiently.

**Impact** – This approach is different from similar programs in other countries because it allows students to graduate without writing a thesis. Instead, they produce real products that companies can use. This helps students gain hands-on experience and makes universities and companies work closely together. It also helps solve important technological challenges in China’s engineering sector.

**What's next step** – The program currently focuses on engineering, but it could expand and inspire other fields. Universities and companies will continue to collaborate, and more students might choose the practical path. This could lead to faster development of new technologies and stronger connections between education and industry.

**One-sentence takeaway** – China’s new practical PhD program lets engineering students graduate by creating real products, bridging education and industry to solve real-world problems.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Chine lance des doctorats pratiques pour stimuler l’innovation en ingénierie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans la plupart des pays, les doctorants doivent rédiger une thèse détaillée pour obtenir leur diplôme. Mais depuis janvier 2025, la Chine permet aux doctorants en ingénierie de terminer leurs études en créant des produits ou projets pratiques au lieu d’écrire une thèse. Ce changement fait partie d’un effort national pour former des ingénieurs d’élite capables de résoudre des problèmes concrets et d’améliorer la technologie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Plus de 60 étudiants ont déjà obtenu leur diplôme grâce à cette nouvelle règle. Ces étudiants étudient à la fois à l’université et en entreprise, encadrés par deux superviseurs – un académique et un expert industriel. Par exemple, Wu Xiangyang, ingénieur ferroviaire, a développé un système pour améliorer la production en usine de véhicules ferroviaires tout en continuant à travailler dans une entreprise de fabrication. Un autre diplômé, Cui Guangzhang, a créé un système informatique intelligent pour faciliter le fonctionnement des programmes d’intelligence artificielle.

**Impact** – Cette approche est différente des programmes similaires dans d’autres pays car elle permet aux étudiants de finir sans rédiger de thèse. Ils produisent plutôt de vrais produits que les entreprises peuvent utiliser. Cela aide les étudiants à acquérir une expérience pratique et renforce la collaboration entre universités et entreprises. Cela contribue aussi à résoudre des défis technologiques importants dans le secteur de l’ingénierie en Chine.

**Prochaine étape** – Le programme se concentre actuellement sur l’ingénierie, mais pourrait s’étendre à d’autres domaines. Les universités et les entreprises continueront de collaborer, et plus d’étudiants pourraient choisir la voie pratique. Cela pourrait accélérer le développement de nouvelles technologies et renforcer les liens entre éducation et industrie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau programme de doctorat pratique de la Chine permet aux étudiants en ingénierie d’obtenir leur diplôme en créant de vrais produits, rapprochant ainsi éducation et industrie pour résoudre des problèmes concrets.
articles.personality_title_es: China lanza doctorados prácticos para impulsar la innovación en ingeniería
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la mayoría de los países, los estudiantes de doctorado deben escribir una tesis detallada para graduarse. Pero desde enero de 2025, China permite que los doctorandos en ingeniería terminen sus estudios creando productos o proyectos prácticos en lugar de escribir una tesis. Este cambio forma parte de un esfuerzo nacional para formar ingenieros de élite que puedan resolver problemas reales y mejorar la tecnología.

**Qué pasó** – Más de 60 estudiantes ya se han graduado bajo esta nueva norma. Estos estudiantes estudian tanto en la universidad como en empresas, guiados por dos supervisores—uno académico y otro experto industrial. Por ejemplo, Wu Xiangyang, un ingeniero ferroviario, desarrolló un sistema para mejorar la producción en fábricas de vehículos ferroviarios mientras seguía trabajando en una empresa manufacturera. Otro graduado, Cui Guangzhang, creó un sistema de computación inteligente para ayudar a ejecutar programas de inteligencia artificial de forma más eficiente.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque es diferente de programas similares en otros países porque permite a los estudiantes graduarse sin escribir una tesis. En cambio, producen productos reales que las empresas pueden usar. Esto ayuda a los estudiantes a obtener experiencia práctica y hace que las universidades y las empresas trabajen más juntas. También ayuda a resolver importantes desafíos tecnológicos en el sector de ingeniería de China.

**Próximo paso** – El programa actualmente se centra en la ingeniería, pero podría expandirse a otros campos. Las universidades y las empresas seguirán colaborando, y más estudiantes podrían elegir la vía práctica. Esto podría acelerar el desarrollo de nuevas tecnologías y fortalecer la conexión entre educación e industria.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo programa de doctorado práctico de China permite que los estudiantes de ingeniería se gradúen creando productos reales, uniendo educación e industria para resolver problemas del mundo real.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate workshop scene with softly glowing mechanical train parts and networked computing devices interconnected by flowing, luminous lines, symbolizing collaborative innovation and practical engineering breakthroughs, rendered in warm, natural tones with a detailed, painterly style.

articles.title: Reinforcement learning control of quantum error correction
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10759-2By integrating reinforcement learning with quantum error correction, a quantum computer continuously self-calibrates during computation, achieving record logical error rates and enhanced resilience to drift.
articles.content:
Next, we demonstrate that RL is not only able to optimize the system performance, but also steer the control policy in the presence of drift. This is achieved by the entropy regularization technique45, which ensures that the policy distribution never becomes deterministic, enabling the agent to continuously explore and adapt to changes in a non-stationary environment. To systematically study this effect, we inject artificial drift on several control parameters simultaneously, as shown in Fig. 4a (red symbols). We choose different temporal drift profiles (step-like, sinusoidal and stroboscopic), different control parameters (CZ coupling strength, XY pulse amplitude and frequency) and different locations of drifting gates on the qubit grid.

Fig. 4: Demonstration of RL steering. Full size image a, The data qubits (yellow diamonds) and measure qubits (panels with data) are arranged in the layout of a distance-5 surface code. We inject artificial drift with various temporal profiles on the gates indicated with red shapes (circles, diamonds and bars) and observe elevated error-detection signals where expected (coloured background). The detection rate associated with each measure qubit is normalized for visualization to remove the effect of the natural system drift. Performance of the fixed control policy (maroon) degrades over time because of the injected drift, whereas RL steering (blue) stabilizes and maintains the error-detection rate (EDR) below its initial level (white lines). b, Time dependence of the injected drift in system control parameters (dashed) and RL steering (solid). An exponential fit of RL response to step-like drift in XY pulse amplitude yields the characteristic learning time of 130 epochs. c, Periodic evaluation of the logical performance indicates that RL steering of the system significantly suppresses and stabilizes the LER, see main text. Incorporating the decoder steering (black) further improves these results.

Following the already-described methodology, we start by calibrating the control policy at t = 0. The performance of this policy (maroon) degrades over time because of the injected drift, as the fixed values of the control parameters become ‘outdated’, leading to additional errors. These control errors lead to an elevated detection event signal, which appears exactly in those detectors in which it is expected based on the constructed factor graph, see tiles highlighted with coloured background in Fig. 4a. In contrast to a fixed control policy, RL steering (blue) maintains a significantly suppressed rate of error-detection events that consistently remains below the initial level (white line), except for brief moments when the drift is too fast. In Fig. 4b, we show the evolution of the learned control parameters. The recovery from a step-like drift in the XY pulse amplitude (red circle) allows us to estimate the response time of the steering process of about 130 epochs. This also sets the time scale for the policy lag in the case of slow continuous drift, as in XY pulse frequency (red diamond).

To confirm that suppression of detection events is not due to hindered detection capability but is due to suppressed errors, we evaluate the logical performance in Fig. 4c. Compared with the fixed policy, we find on average a 24% reduction of LER and a 2.4× improvement of its stability (quantified here by the standard deviation of the LER distribution). These figures of merit further improve to 31% and 3.5×, respectively, by additionally steering the decoder parameters. Decoder steering is achieved within the same RL framework by reweighting the matching graph as described in ref. 46. Although the steering of the classical controller is done via the surrogate objective C that relies exclusively on the error-detection probabilities, our decoder steering process relies on LER estimation, which is not straightforwardly scalable to the real-time setting. However, alternative approaches to decoder steering have been proposed47,48,49 that, in principle, do not suffer from this limitation.

We also analysed the RL performance under natural system drift, which arises from sources ranging from material defects near the quantum system50 to temperature fluctuations in the classical control instruments and, unlike our previous demonstration, rarely has a simple time dependence. Fourier analysis of multiple experimental RL runs from Fig. 3a shows that the effect of steering can be understood as a filter that provides about 4 dB of suppression of low-frequency LER fluctuations originating from these natural sources (Supplementary Information section III).

We have thus far demonstrated that the mean μ(t) of the Gaussian policy distribution learns to track the optimal policy over time in the presence of drift. As a result, the learned policy μ(t) outperforms the fixed policy, with lower EDR and LER. However, as the learning process requires exploration of the parameter space, the algorithm inevitably samples policy candidates whose performance is worse than that of the μ(t) policy. This ‘exploration noise’ is irrelevant in our experimental setting in which RL steering relies on repeated executions of a short logical algorithm and the quantum state is independently re-prepared in every shot. However, in the future, this steering must be done in real time during the single-shot execution of a long logical algorithm. In that case, the exploration noise, although necessary for learning, will be detrimental to the performance of the logical algorithm. Generally, balancing the exploration of parameter space and the exploitation of the learned policy μ(t) is a central challenge in many applications of RL51. In our case, the favourable resolution of the exploration–exploitation trade-off would mean that the aggregate performance of all sampled policy candidates, most of which are worse than μ(t), is still better than the performance without RL steering.

To study this trade-off, we conducted numerical simulations of real-time steering of the distance-3 surface code subject to sinusoidal parameter drift at different frequencies (Fig. 5a and Supplementary Information section VIA). We count the total number of error-detection events generated in a 1.8 × 109-cycle window of QEC, and normalize it so that level 1 corresponds to the performance of the optimal policy (known in the simulation), and level 0 corresponds to the performance of a fixed policy (optimal at t = 0). We control the exploration–exploitation trade-off by changing the amount of entropy regularization45 in our RL algorithm. Our findings indicate that there is a critical drift frequency, about 1/150 epochs, below which the system becomes real-time steerable: the performance with exploration noise is better than the performance of a fixed policy. This critical frequency is consistent with the response time of the learning algorithm observed in the experiment (Fig. 4b). When drift is too fast, the real-time steering is not able to keep up—this drift must be mitigated at the hardware level. In particular, correlated drift caused by rare high-energy particle impacts in superconducting devices8,41 typically equilibrates on a much shorter time scale and is not real-time steerable in our current implementation. By contrast, at low drift frequencies, the exploration and exploitation can be successfully balanced to closely approach the performance of the optimal policy at all times.

Fig. 5: Real-time steering and scaling simulations. Full size image a, Normalized improvement (colour) of error-detection rate in the real-time steering simulation of the distance-3 surface code subject to sinusoidal drift at different frequencies. Level 1 indicates the performance of the optimal policy. Isoline at level 0 demarcates the boundary beyond which real-time steering results in better performance than a fixed policy, approaching the performance of an optimal policy in the regime of slow drift. b, Simulation of scalability of RL control of large surface codes. The algorithm learns the parameters of single-qubit and CZ gates, with 30 control parameters per gate, amounting to almost 40,000 control parameters in total for the distance-15 code. During the learning process, the LER reduces over time (colour) until it reaches the floor (red bars) set by the irreducible physical error rates and characterized by the optimal error suppression factor Λ*. c, Point estimates of Λ at every code distance and learning epoch from b confirm that the speed, ∂ t Λ/Λ* × 102, at which the error suppression factor approaches the local optimum, is proportional to the distance from the optimum, 1 − Λ/Λ*. The convergence rate γ (see main text) is independent of the system size but depends on the number of control parameters per gate, with three beams corresponding to 1, 10 and 30 parameters, and the linear fits (red) indicating the convergence rates.

Thus, our simulations establish that RL is able to effectively use the information concealed in the error-detection events to calibrate the system while continuing the logical computation. This ability offers a substantial advantage over approaches based on the synthesis of traditional calibration and code deformation21, because RL steering does not introduce any resource overhead.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in quantum computing by integrating reinforcement learning with quantum error correction, enabling continuous self-calibration and improved error resilience. This advancement has broad implications for the future reliability and scalability of quantum computers, which can impact many fields reliant on quantum technology. The article provides detailed experimental and simulation results, demonstrating measurable improvements in logical error rates and system stability, thus meeting the criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: New AI method helps quantum computers fix errors while running
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers use special codes to fix errors during calculations, but their parts can change over time, causing mistakes. Keeping these systems stable is a big challenge.

**What happened** – Scientists combined a type of artificial intelligence called reinforcement learning (RL) with quantum error correction. This AI watches the computer as it works and adjusts settings automatically to fix errors caused by changes in the system. They tested this by adding different kinds of changes, like shifts in signal strength, and showed the AI could keep the error rates low and stable.

**Impact** – This is important because it means quantum computers can correct themselves continuously without stopping. The AI reduced errors by about 24% and made the system more stable, sometimes improving stability by over three times. This makes quantum computers more reliable and closer to practical use.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to improve this AI control to work in real time during longer calculations and handle faster changes. They also want to test it on bigger quantum systems with more parts to see if it still works well.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using AI to control quantum error correction helps quantum computers fix mistakes on their own, making them more reliable and ready for real-world tasks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle méthode d'IA aide les ordinateurs quantiques à corriger leurs erreurs en temps réel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques utilisent des codes spéciaux pour corriger les erreurs pendant les calculs, mais leurs composants peuvent changer avec le temps, ce qui cause des fautes. Maintenir ces systèmes stables est un grand défi.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont combiné un type d'intelligence artificielle appelé apprentissage par renforcement (RL) avec la correction d'erreurs quantiques. Cette IA surveille l'ordinateur pendant qu'il fonctionne et ajuste automatiquement les réglages pour corriger les erreurs dues aux changements du système. Ils ont testé cela en introduisant différents types de variations, comme des changements de force du signal, et ont montré que l'IA pouvait garder les taux d'erreur faibles et stables.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela signifie que les ordinateurs quantiques peuvent se corriger en continu sans s'arrêter. L'IA a réduit les erreurs d'environ 24 % et a rendu le système plus stable, parfois en améliorant la stabilité par plus de trois fois. Cela rend les ordinateurs quantiques plus fiables et plus proches d'une utilisation pratique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'améliorer ce contrôle IA pour qu'il fonctionne en temps réel pendant des calculs plus longs et puisse gérer des changements plus rapides. Ils veulent aussi tester cela sur des systèmes quantiques plus grands avec plus de composants pour vérifier son efficacité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Utiliser l'IA pour contrôler la correction d'erreurs quantiques aide les ordinateurs quantiques à corriger leurs erreurs eux-mêmes, les rendant plus fiables et prêts pour des tâches réelles.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de IA ayuda a las computadoras cuánticas a corregir errores en tiempo real
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas usan códigos especiales para corregir errores durante los cálculos, pero sus partes pueden cambiar con el tiempo, causando fallos. Mantener estos sistemas estables es un gran desafío.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos combinaron un tipo de inteligencia artificial llamada aprendizaje por refuerzo (RL) con la corrección de errores cuánticos. Esta IA observa la computadora mientras funciona y ajusta automáticamente las configuraciones para corregir errores causados por cambios en el sistema. Lo probaron añadiendo diferentes tipos de variaciones, como cambios en la fuerza de la señal, y mostraron que la IA podía mantener los niveles de error bajos y estables.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque significa que las computadoras cuánticas pueden corregirse solas continuamente sin detenerse. La IA redujo los errores en alrededor del 24 % y hizo el sistema más estable, a veces mejorando la estabilidad más de tres veces. Esto hace que las computadoras cuánticas sean más confiables y estén más cerca de un uso práctico.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean mejorar este control de IA para que funcione en tiempo real durante cálculos más largos y pueda manejar cambios más rápidos. También quieren probarlo en sistemas cuánticos más grandes con más partes para ver si sigue funcionando bien.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usar IA para controlar la corrección de errores cuánticos ayuda a las computadoras cuánticas a corregir sus errores por sí mismas, haciéndolas más confiables y listas para tareas reales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing network of interconnected diamond-shaped nodes and panels arranged in a precise grid, with gentle waves and pulses of light flowing through them symbolizing adaptive control; scattered red markers represent challenges or drift, while a calm blue aura envelops the network, illustrating reinforcement learning’s steady guidance and error correction in a dynamic quantum system.

articles.title: Special relativity can warp chemical bonds – now we've seen it happen
articles.summary: An experiment with a charged molecule of bismuth and carbon reveals how effects from Albert Einstein’s special relativity reshape the standard understanding of chemical bonds
articles.content:
An experiment with a charged molecule of bismuth and carbon reveals how effects from Albert Einstein’s special relativity reshape the standard understanding of chemical bonds

In some heavy atoms, like those of bismuth (pictured in crystalline form), electrons move at relativistic speeds savva_25/Shutterstock

Albert Einstein’s theory of special relativity can reshape chemical bonds within molecules, and researchers have just seen it happen for the first time.

The theory of special relativity describes how moving at speeds close to the speed of light would affect travellers’ experience of space and time. Because of this, it is usually associated with particle accelerators and spacefaring objects, but within some heavy atoms, electrons experience relativistic speeds too.

Advertisement

Lai-Sheng Wang at Brown University in Rhode Island and his colleagues have now managed to take an unprecedented look at how this breaks the standard notion of chemical bonds within a charged molecule made from bismuth and carbon.

Within the molecule, a bismuth atom and a carbon atom were connected by three bonds, one of which the researchers expected to be of “sigma” type and two of “pi” type. The difference between these two types stems from electrons’ quantum character – each electron is “smeared” across some region of space, instead of being a tight ball, and whether these regions overlap head on or side by side determines the type of chemical bond they create between the atoms.

In their experiment, Wang and his colleagues mapped the distribution of electrons throughout the molecule, effectively getting a look at its bonds. But instead of seeing electrons distributed in shapes associated with sigma and pi bonds, the team noticed that two of the bonds resembled two different mixes of sigma and pi shapes. “Their characters are different from our normal understanding,” says Wang. “You can’t really call it the sigma and pi.”

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

His team turned to Kirk Peterson at Washington State University, whose calculations ultimately showed that this mixing was a consequence of electrons near the bismuth nucleus feeling such a strong electromagnetic interaction that they moved at relativistic speeds. He says this effect hadn’t previously been captured in an experiment.

“The hardest thing about [studying] heavy elements is a lack of really good experimental data,” says Peterson. “To have such a beautiful experiment to be able to essentially compare very high-level theory to data is really a luxury.”

Wang says one important part of the new experiment is that he and his colleagues could make the molecule very cold before looking at its electrons. This dampened any jitters and excitations that would have made the final images imprecise.

“The methods they have used, both experimental and theoretical, are the best possible ones,” says Pekka Pyykkö at the University of Helsinki in Finland.

He says this relativistic reshaping of bismuth’s bonding with carbon could influence how organic bismuth compounds are used in chemical reactions. In fact, a recent study by researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Coal Research in Germany has already shown that relativistic effects help make this heavy metal a good catalyst of chemical processes.

Wang says the researchers now want to repeat their experiment but swap bismuth for elements close to it in the periodic table, so they can see when exactly special relativity makes the traditional chemical bond structure collapse.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers experimentally observed the influence of special relativity on chemical bonds in heavy atoms. This discovery reshapes fundamental understanding in chemistry with potential broad implications for catalysis and chemical reactions, representing a meaningful advancement in science and technology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe Einstein’s relativity changing chemical bonds in heavy atoms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Albert Einstein’s special relativity theory explains how moving near the speed of light affects space and time. Usually, this idea is linked to fast-moving objects like spacecraft or particles. But inside very heavy atoms like bismuth, some electrons move so fast that relativity matters.

**What happened** – Researchers at Brown University studied a charged molecule made of bismuth and carbon atoms. They expected to see usual chemical bonds called sigma and pi bonds between these atoms. Instead, the electrons formed new mixed types of bonds. This happened because electrons near the bismuth nucleus moved at relativistic speeds, changing how bonds look.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have directly seen special relativity change chemical bonds in an experiment. It challenges the traditional way chemists understand bonding. Understanding these changes can help improve how heavy elements like bismuth are used in chemical reactions and as catalysts, which speed up important processes.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to try the same experiment with elements similar to bismuth to find out exactly when relativity starts to alter chemical bonds. This could lead to new discoveries about how heavy atoms behave and help design better chemicals and materials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have experimentally shown that Einstein’s special relativity changes chemical bonds in heavy atoms, opening new paths in chemistry research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques observent la relativité d’Einstein modifiant les liaisons chimiques dans les atomes lourds
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La théorie de la relativité restreinte d’Albert Einstein explique comment se déplacer près de la vitesse de la lumière affecte l’espace et le temps. Habituellement, cette idée concerne des objets rapides comme les engins spatiaux ou les particules. Mais dans des atomes très lourds comme le bismuth, certains électrons vont si vite que la relativité a un effet.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Brown ont étudié une molécule chargée composée d’atomes de bismuth et de carbone. Ils s’attendaient à voir des liaisons chimiques classiques appelées liaisons sigma et pi entre ces atomes. Mais les électrons ont formé de nouveaux types de liaisons mélangées. Cela s’explique parce que les électrons près du noyau de bismuth se déplacent à des vitesses relativistes, modifiant la forme des liaisons.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des scientifiques observent directement que la relativité restreinte modifie les liaisons chimiques dans une expérience. Cela remet en question la compréhension traditionnelle des liaisons en chimie. Comprendre ces changements peut améliorer l’utilisation des éléments lourds comme le bismuth dans les réactions chimiques et comme catalyseurs, qui accélèrent des processus importants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de refaire l’expérience avec des éléments proches du bismuth pour savoir exactement quand la relativité commence à modifier les liaisons chimiques. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouvelles découvertes sur le comportement des atomes lourds et aider à concevoir de meilleurs produits chimiques et matériaux.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont montré expérimentalement que la relativité restreinte d’Einstein modifie les liaisons chimiques dans les atomes lourds, ouvrant de nouvelles voies en recherche chimique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan que la relatividad de Einstein cambia los enlaces químicos en átomos pesados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La teoría de la relatividad especial de Albert Einstein explica cómo moverse cerca de la velocidad de la luz afecta el espacio y el tiempo. Normalmente, esta idea se asocia con objetos rápidos como naves espaciales o partículas. Pero dentro de átomos muy pesados como el bismuto, algunos electrones se mueven tan rápido que la relatividad tiene efecto.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Brown estudiaron una molécula cargada formada por átomos de bismuto y carbono. Esperaban ver enlaces químicos normales llamados sigma y pi entre estos átomos. En cambio, los electrones formaron nuevos tipos mezclados de enlaces. Esto ocurrió porque los electrones cerca del núcleo de bismuto se movían a velocidades relativistas, cambiando la forma de los enlaces.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven directamente que la relatividad especial cambia los enlaces químicos en un experimento. Esto desafía la forma tradicional en que los químicos entienden los enlaces. Comprender estos cambios puede ayudar a mejorar el uso de elementos pesados como el bismuto en reacciones químicas y como catalizadores, que aceleran procesos importantes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean repetir el experimento con elementos similares al bismuto para descubrir cuándo exactamente la relatividad comienza a alterar los enlaces químicos. Esto podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos sobre el comportamiento de átomos pesados y ayudar a diseñar mejores productos químicos y materiales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos han demostrado experimentalmente que la relatividad especial de Einstein cambia los enlaces químicos en átomos pesados, abriendo nuevas vías en la investigación química.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an abstract molecular structure combining crystalline bismuth and carbon atoms, where glowing electron clouds swirl and blend in unusual overlapping shapes symbolizing mixed sigma and pi bonds, set against a soft, neutral background that evokes a calm laboratory atmosphere.
